response:
# Top 3 changes to reduce risk in `src/billing.ts`

## 1. Break up `generateInvoice` (lines 50–253)
**Tied to:** `crime_00002` — God Function, high severity, 3.4× the 60-line threshold.

204 lines is the dominant risk in this file. It almost certainly bundles several responsibilities (the file also carries 7 `Date.now()`/`new Date()` calls per `crime_00009`, scattered across lines 57–273, which suggests time-handling, line-item math, and side effects are interleaved). An agent editing the proration section can silently break the tax/rounding/email section because nothing in the function structure tells it where the seams are. Extract cohesive sections — `buildLineItems`, `applyProration`, `finalizeInvoice` — so each section can be read in isolation and tested without spinning up the whole pipeline. This also shrinks the file under the 310-line `crime_00016` threshold as a side effect.

## 2. Rename or split `calculateRefundPreview` (lines 40–44)
**Tied to:** `crime_00013` — False Identity, plus the orphan rule in `crime_00017`.

The name says "preview" (read-only, safe to call from a UI handler), but the body calls `saveRefundAudit` and `sendRefundEmail` — and the comment on line 39 ("Only owners can refund enterprise plans unless billing support approves") encodes a policy that isn't visible in the call signature. This is the highest-leverage agent risk in the file: an agent wiring up a refund confirmation modal will reasonably call `calculateRefundPreview` to populate the preview number and unintentionally email the customer and write an audit row. Split into a pure `calculateRefundAmount(...)` plus an explicit `commitRefund(...)` that owns the side effects, and lift the comment's rule into a typed `canRefund(actor, plan)` guard so it can't be missed.

## 3. Inject a clock to retire the 7 direct `Date` calls
**Tied to:** `crime_00009` — Temporal Recklessness in billing.ts, reinforced by `crime_00011` and `crime_00030` showing the existing billing tests assert nothing.

Seven `Date.now()` / `new Date()` calls in invoice generation means proration windows, due-date math, and audit timestamps all read the wall clock at call time. With the test suite already weak (one zero-assert test, one with only weak matchers), there's no safety net to catch a regression introduced when you do (1) and (2). Threading a `clock: () => Date` parameter (or a module-level injected `now()`) through `generateInvoice` is cheap, unblocks meaningful tests on the extracted helpers, and lets you delete the cross-cutting `crime_00009` finding in one pass rather than chasing each `Date.now()` individually after the split.

**Order matters:** do (3) before (1) so the extracted helpers can be tested deterministically as you pull them out, and do (2) standalone — it doesn't depend on the split and the misleading-name risk is too sharp to leave sitting while you refactor around it.
